summary: Pristine audience recording of an electrifying 1981 MSG show – standout China‑Cat > Rider, The Other One and soaring Bird Song with crystal‑clear sound.
review: The performance is consistently high‑energy from start to finish, highlighted by a monster China‑Cat > Rider sequence, incendiary The Other One, and tight jams on Samson & Delilah, Deep Elem Blues and Estimated > Uncle John’s Band.  Glassberg’s audience tape captures the MSG room with exceptional clarity and spatial depth, making it one of the finest AUDs of the era.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 40 reviews (mostly 5‑star) praise both the musical performance and the recording quality; the show is repeatedly called a top‑tier 1981 concert, while the audience tape is described as pristine, spacious and comparable to soundboard sources.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Pristine, crisp, with strong room ambience; reviewers note superb spatial imaging, no noticeable speed‑up, and a mix that lets Jerry’s guitar pierce through while keeping the audience buzz intact. A few minor comments about occasional tape quirks but overall flawless.

show_quality.setlist_flow: High‑octane first set that builds into an explosive second set; seamless transitions especially China > Rider and Estimated > UJB.  Energy stays elevated throughout, with the band feeding off a wildly responsive MSG crowd.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Repeatedly praised for ripping guitar tones, perfect phrasing on China Cat, soaring solos on Rider and The Other One; described as ‘piercing’ and ‘perfect’.
band_member_comments.Phil: Noted for solid, driving bass lines that “pound” especially during The Other One and the jam sections.
band_member_comments.Bob: Mentioned mainly for a brief Frampton‑like opening lick on China Cat and occasional rhythm work; no major criticism.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent’s synth/guitar solo on Minglewood highlighted as a unique, cat‑in‑heat sound; keyboards overall described as solid with Brent finding his place in the band.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill/Mickey’s drumming called ‘blistering’ and a key driver of the show’s relentless momentum.
